title: Protecting Human Rights at the World Cup: Progress on Paper, Gaps on the Ground
gist: FIFA's post-2016 human rights policy has produced better paperwork but not proportionally better outcomes — Qatar's 2022 Cup still saw independent death-toll estimates run above 6,500 versus FIFA's own tally of 40, and the same accountability gaps threaten to recur, with FIFA already rating Saudi Arabia's accepted future bid as only 'medium' human rights risk.

themes.theme: How FIFA got a human rights policy

insights.claim: FIFA took on no direct responsibility for human rights conditions around its events until 2016, leaving treatment entirely to host governments; the shift began after a 2014 letter from former UN High Commissioner for Human Rights Mary Robinson and scholar John Ruggie urged FIFA to commit to human rights, and pressure compounded when a 2015 FBI investigation resulted in the indictment of nine FIFA officials and five corporate executives for what the DOJ called "rampant, systemic, and deep-rooted" corruption.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Shows FIFA's human rights turn was reactive to scandal and external pressure, not proactive — a pattern worth watching for whether future policy tightening is similarly scandal-driven rather than preventive.
insights.locator: Q1: How does FIFA approach human rights?

insights.claim: FIFA's May 2017 Human Rights Policy defined five focus areas (labor rights, land acquisition and housing rights, discrimination, security, players' rights) and a four-part approach (commit and embed, identify and address, protect and remedy, engage and communicate); since October 2017, World Cup bidding countries must commit to the UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights and submit host-city Human Rights Action Plans — but because the policy postdates their bids, the 2018 Russia and 2022 Qatar World Cups were never bound by it.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Explains the specific mechanism (bid-cycle timing) that let two of the most controversial recent World Cups escape FIFA's own human rights requirements entirely.
insights.locator: The policy was initially operationalized in October 2017

insights.claim: Beautification and rental-housing projects for South Africa's 2010 World Cup forcibly displaced several thousand people, and roughly 170,000 people were evicted ahead of Brazil's 2014 tournament to clear space for World Cup and 2016 Olympic construction, with Brazil's Cup also coinciding with nationwide anti-spending protests met by tear gas, rubber bullets and mass arrests.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Establishes the baseline pattern of unmitigated host-country abuse that FIFA's 2016 policy was designed to address.
insights.locator: During the 2010 World Cup in South Africa

themes.theme: Policy on paper, abuse on the ground: Russia 2018 and Qatar 2022

insights.claim: FIFA's post-2016 policy record does not match its stated commitments: Russia's 2018 World Cup construction killed at least 21 workers, and investigators found at least 110 North Korean workers subjected to forced labor at the Zenit Arena in St. Petersburg (one found dead in a storage container), while Russian authorities detained a Human Rights Watch researcher trying to interview workers and denied workers contact with a FIFA delegation they wanted to raise wage concerns with.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Documents a case where the host state actively suppressed the monitoring mechanisms FIFA's own policy was supposed to enable.
insights.locator: Russia's hosting included significant labor abuses

insights.claim: Qatar's officially reported World Cup stadium death toll was just 3 work-related and 37 non-work-related deaths, while independent estimates of deaths connected to World Cup preparations run above 6,500 — despite Qatar introducing a formal Human Rights Grievance Mechanism and Human Rights Volunteers program that FIFA's policy encouraged.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The gap between official and independent death tolls (40 vs. 6,500+) is the single starkest data point showing FIFA's formal mechanisms did not translate into accurate accountability, let alone prevention.

insights.claim: Qatar's kafala labor system, which prevents migrant workers from changing jobs or leaving the country without employer permission, persisted through World Cup preparation and execution despite FIFA's stated labor rights commitments, showing that formal policy language does not by itself change host-country structural labor law.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Illustrates that FIFA-level commitments are insufficient without host-country legal reform — a template for judging future bids' credibility.

insights.claim: The 2019 Women's World Cup in France, whose bid was awarded in 2015 (also before FIFA's human rights policy took effect), had a markedly better labor rights, women's rights and migrant worker protection record than Russia 2018 or Qatar 2022, despite facing no additional FIFA-imposed human rights requirements.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Suggests host-country baseline institutions and rule of law predict human rights outcomes better than FIFA policy language does — a signal for evaluating the credibility of any future host bid regardless of FIFA's stated requirements.
insights.locator: The Women's World Cup in France constitutes a distinct contrast

themes.theme: Why 2026 looks different, and where it doesn't

insights.claim: FIFA's tightened approach for 2026 is attributed to the compounding weight of civil-society and media pressure following Qatar and Russia, plus the fact that the 2026 hosts (United States, Mexico, Canada) have comparatively robust transparency, accountability and public procurement laws relative to Russia or Qatar — a combination the authors say created a rare window where the international community had already seen the worst-case outcomes before a tournament landed in countries with both means and motivation to do better.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Implies the 2026 improvement may be host-country-specific rather than a durable FIFA-wide reform, raising doubt about whether gains persist for future non-democratic hosts.
insights.locator: Q3: Why is FIFA taking action now?

insights.claim: A FIFA-commissioned report on human rights violations tied to Qatar's 2022 World Cup was delivered to FIFA in 2023 but not published until 2024; it found that workers entitled to remediation had not yet received it and that responsibility fell partly on FIFA itself — yet the Qatar Legacy Fund, financed by 2022 World Cup revenue, is being spent on general international development projects rather than direct worker remediation.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Shows that even when culpability and violations are formally established, real financial repercussions for FIFA remain limited — a warning sign for the credibility of any future remediation promises.
insights.locator: FIFA commissioned a report on the human rights violations

insights.claim: FIFA rated Saudi Arabia's accepted future World Cup bid as only "medium" human rights risk despite the country's well-documented history of prominent human rights violations and its regular presence near the bottom of global human rights and freedom rankings.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A concrete forward-looking red flag: FIFA's own risk-rating framework appears to understate risk for its next authoritarian host, undermining confidence that 2026's relative improvement will carry forward.

insights.claim: Closing civic space and a growing willingness to host major sporting events in authoritarian settings makes it harder for local civil-society partners — who international rights organizations depend on for ground-level monitoring — to operate; Russian researcher Semyon Simonov faced legal jeopardy after doing vital human and labor rights reporting ahead of the 2014 Sochi Olympics and 2018 World Cup.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Identifies local civil-society access, not just FIFA policy, as the binding constraint on monitoring quality for future authoritarian-state hosts like Saudi Arabia in 2034.

insights.claim: All 16 host cities for the 2026 World Cup have reportedly submitted the FIFA-required Human Rights Action Plans, but there is no uniform standard for publication or public availability of these plans, and this comes amid ongoing controversy over the right to protest and free speech, travel restrictions, mass deportations, labor rights, and housing displacement in the host countries.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Undercuts any narrative that 2026 has "solved" World Cup human rights risk — formal compliance (submitted plans) coexists with an active, unresolved set of rights controversies on the ground.
insights.locator: While FIFA has stated that all 16 host cities

insights.claim: Three factors will determine the trajectory of human rights protection at future World Cups: sustained media and civil-society attention (likely to continue), whether FIFA imposes real transparent and enforceable costs on host countries for violations (evidence so far is troubling), and whether host countries themselves treat rights protection as a required cost of hosting rather than optional (early evidence does not inspire optimism).
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete three-part scorecard for assessing whether future World Cup bids (e.g., Saudi Arabia 2034) will actually protect human rights or merely replicate the paperwork-without-enforcement pattern.
insights.locator: Q4: What does this mean for the future?
